data.title: Short sellers targeting major auto stocks, growing their positions - S3 Partners
data.description:
It's been a volatile year for the global automotive industry as it has been beset by falling demand amid higher interest rates. A report earlier this week found that the number of people willing to bet against major global auto stocks has been steadily rising.

Several global auto stocks are facing increasing short positions and analyst sell recommendations, S3 Partners' research team said on Thursday.

"Notable patterns emerge across regions, with larger companies like Toyota, Volvo, and Porsche showing a rise in short interest and sell ratings, while smaller firms exhibit persistent short positions with minimal shifts in analyst sentiment," the team said.

According to S3, European luxury car markers Volvo (OTCPK:VLVCY) and Volkswagen's (OTCPK:VWAGY) Porsche have seen their short positions double YTD. Japan's Toyota (TM) has experienced a similar increase in short interest.

Meanwhile, in the U.S., S3 highlighted four companies in particular for their notable short positions: Lucid (LCID), Rivian Automotive (RIVN), Winnebago (WGO), and Nio (NIO).

See S3's chart below:

"For the major stocks in each region, the short position has grown as the stock has fallen. For the smaller stocks, many had large short positions to begin with," S3's research team said.

"Porsche and Nissan follow a reversal pattern, with shorting on the way up and covering on the way down. Volvo and WGO show a similar trend, though with a weaker relationship. NIO follows a momentum strategy, with shorting on the way down; RIVN is less pronounced in this pattern," the team added.

Nissan (OTCPK:NSANY) and Japanese peer Honda (HMC) sent reverberations through the industry this week after chatter of the two being in merger talks.

The global auto industry has been grappling with slowing demand as consumers pull back on discretionary spending amid high inflation and tight monetary policy.

"Globally, auto sales in the first half of 2024 slowed down compared to the rapid rebound seen in 2023. Sales volumes in 2024 are now expected to reach 91.4M units, just a 1.8% increase over the 89.8M units sold in 2023. Sales have slowed globally due to weaker customer demand, mixed economic conditions and higher interest rates," risk and financial advisory firm Kroll said last month.

China remained the automotive king in 2024. According to S&P Global Mobility's auto demand tracker, Greater China comprised nearly 28% of November's global demand volume of 7.332M.

Here is the YTD movement for several major auto stocks -

  • Europe: Stellantis (STLA) -44.7%; Volvo (OTCPK:VLVCY) -35.4%; Volkswagen (OTCPK:VWAGY) -27.5%; BMW (OTCPK:BAMXF) -27.3%; Mercedes-Benz (OTCPK:MBGAF) -19.4%.
  • U.S.: Tesla (TSLA) +69.5%; General Motors (GM) +44.2%; Ford (F) -18.9%; Rivian (RIVN) -41%.
  • China: Geely Automobile (OTCPK:GELYF) +75.9%; BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF) +24.3%; XPeng (XPEV) -13.4%; Li Auto (LI) -37.1%; Nio (NIO) -49.9%.
  • Japan: Toyota (TM) -2.8%; Honda (HMC) -22.7%; Nissan (OTCPK:NSANY) -27.5%; Mazda (OTCPK:MZDAY) -41.5%.

Here are some exchange-traded funds of interest that track the automotive and electric vehicle industries: (TSLL), (TSLQ), (TSLS), (CARZ), (FDRV), (VCAR), (CARU), and (DRIV).

Related stories

  • Volvo Cars sales rise 5% Y/Y in November
  • Volkswagen: Restructuring In Place Means Short-Term Pain And Long-Term Gain
  • EU new car registrations fall 1.9% in November Y/Y
  • The hottest auto stock over the last six weeks is not Tesla
  • Stellantis Needs To Put America First - A Tough Sell To Its European Shareholders

data.title: Italian regulators approve Swisscom's $8.34B takeover of Vodafone Italia
Wall Street's take on the regional banking sector for 2025
data.description:
It's that time of year when Wall Street releases its sector outlooks, and with President-elect Donald Trump promising a friendlier regulatory environment, regional banks look poised for a bullish 2025.

Even though regional banking stocks have been benefiting from Trump 2.0, the SPDR S&P Regional Banking ETF (NYSEARCA:KRE), which tracks the performance of regional lenders, is on pace to end the year some 7% percentage points below the broader S&P 500.

For Piper Sandler, "we believe the focus will center on 2025 guidance given the potential for stronger revenue growth and positive EPS estimate revisions," analyst Nathan Race wrote in a Dec. 16 note to clients.

Stoking his outlook is the recent shift back to a positively sloping yield curve, where long-term rates surpass short-term rates. Banks typically borrow money at short-dated rates and lend it out at long-dated rates. Generally, when the yield curve disinverts, the difference between the rates at which banks borrow and lend widens, allowing them to charge higher rates on loans while paying lower rates on deposits or short-term borrowing.

"With election-driven regulatory relief presumably on the horizon and recent expansion in valuations/currencies, we also anticipate M&A prospects to be a focal point during 4Q earnings season discussions," the analyst added.

Similarly, J.P. Morgan's Anthony Elian remains "encouraged on the direction of regional banks heading into 2025," he wrote in a Dec. 17 note.

Elian sees improved prospects for fundamentals in loan and deposit growth and net interest margin expansion. Another reason for his optimism is "the fact that dozens of generalists and long-only investors that we have spoken to since the election are showing increasing interest in the group but have not yet pulled the buy trigger en masse."

Despite the positive outlook, he pointed out, bank stocks in his coverage universe still trade at a relative discount, changing hands at 1.5x 2025 estimated tangible book value (vs. historical valuation range of 1.8x-2.0x). Seeking Alpha's Quant system gives the highest rating among regionals to Third Coast Bancshares (TCBX), followed by First Savings Financial Group (FSFG), Northeast Community Bancorp (NECB), and M&T Bank (MTB).

The next leg higher in banking stocks will likely be driven by: "(1) tangible proposals on what the Trump administration intends to carry out, (2) actually seeing signs of industry loan growth come back with several banks at a recent conference pointing optimism in 2025, (3) deregulation including clarity on what action (if any) is taken on Basel III Endgame, and (4) M&A coming back including the first few deals of size out of the gate," Elian said.

His top regional bank picks for 2025 include Western Alliance Bancorporation (WAL), First Citizens BancShares (FCNCA) and Pinnacle Financial Partners (PNFP).

Stanley Druckenmiller's Duquesne Family Office loaded into regional banking stocks in Q3, which ended Sept. 30, 2024.

Heading into the new year, Raymond James analyst Michael Rose said he favors banks that stand to gain the most from a deregulatory environment, positive operating leverage and a steeper yield curve.

Barclays analyst Jared Shaw's 2025 banking outlook echo much of what his sell-side peers have outlined: a favorable macroeconomic environment for growth and M&A, coupled with NIM optimism.

"We think with the potential for several quarters of results reflecting improving macro backdrops, investors will continue to move into the space," according to his Dec. 19 note. He added that regional banks' capital exposure to the battered commercial real estate market is a headwind that will ease through next year.

Related tickers: U.S. Bancorp (USB), PNC Financial Services Group (PNC), Flagstar Financial (FLG), Axos Financial (AX), KeyCorp (KEY), Regions Financial (RF), Huntington Bancshares (HBAN), Truist Financial (TFC), Comerica (CMA), Citizens Financial Group (CFG), Zions Bancorporation (ZION).

ETFs: (DPST), (IAT), (KBWR), (SKRE).

data.title: OpenAI project to improve ChatGPT behind schedule as costs soar: report
Key deals for the week: Accenture, Berkshire Hills Bancorp, NXP Semiconductors and more
data.description:
A quick look at a list of key deals reported this week across sectors:

McCormick (NYSE:MKC) is in discussions to purchase Sauer Brands, best known for its Duke's mayonnaise brand, for more than $1 billion.

Accenture (NYSE:ACN) to acquire the provider of engineering managed services for large infrastructure projects, IQT.

Bank holding companies Berkshire Hills Bancorp (NYSE:BHLB) and Brookline Bancorp (NASDAQ:BRKL) announced on Monday an all-stock merger transaction, valued at ~$1.1B.

ASSA ABLOY (OTCPK:ASAZY) has acquired Norshield Security Products, a U.S. manufacturer of high-security openings and enclosures, for an undisclosed sum.

Bank holding company Northwest Bancshares (NASDAQ:NWBI) is set to acquire Penns Woods Bancorp (NASDAQ:PWOD) in an all-stock transaction valued at ~$270.4M.

Private equity firm TPG (NASDAQ:TPG) is in advanced discussions to acquire Crown Castle's (NYSE:CCI) fiber unit for about $8 billion. Shares of Crown Castle rose 7% in after hours trading.

Italy's UniCredit (OTCPK:UNCFF) increased its stake in Commerzbank (OTCPK:CRZBF) to 28%, utilizing additional derivatives. This move is part of a broader strategy to potentially acquire up to 29.9% of the German bank.

NXP Semiconductors (NASDAQ:NXPI) has agreed to acquire in-vehicle connectivity solutions provider Aviva Links in an all-cash transaction valued at $242.5M.

Lumen (NYSE:LUMN) rose 5% on Thursday after a story confirming that the company has started a sale process for the consumer fiber operations and the business may be worth $6 billion to $9 billion in a transaction.

WM Technology (MAPS) has received a non-binding proposal from co-founders Douglas Francis (CEO and Chairman) and Justin Hartfield to acquire all outstanding common stock not already owned by them at $1.70 per share.

Trane Technologies (NYSE:TT) on Wednesday said it will acquire BrainBox AI. BrainBox AI's is a tech company that uses deep learning, cloud computing and custom algorithms.

HashiCorp (NASDAQ:HCP) gained after a report about Federal Trade Commission's review of its planned $6.4 billion sale to IBM (NYSE:IBM).

Novo Holdings said that it completed its $16.5 billion acquisition of Catalent (CTLT)- a provider of delivery technologies, development, drug manufacturing, biologics, gene therapies and consumer health products..

data.title: 14 out of 16 companies deliver EPS wins this week-Earnings Scorecard
data.description:
Wall Street found it difficult to retain its short-lived bounce back after seeing its worst session in over three months, as investors grew concerned about the state of the U.S. economy. The market, however, pared some losses on Friday, after November’s core PCE report came in cooler than expected.

The Federal Reserve’s favored inflation gauge-core PCE- ticked down in November, and edged up 0.1% M/M, less than the +0.2% consensus.

The Wall Street took one of its worst hits in over three months after the Federal Reserve’s hawkish interest rate cut this week. The Fed on Wednesday delivered a 25 basis point rate cut, which was widely expected, but the central bank’s updated dot plot was what kept the investors on their toes.

The Fed now sees higher inflation and fewer rate cuts in 2025, with Jerome Powell stressing caution going forward.

To add to investors’ worries, a stopgap spending plan backed by President-elect Donald Trump was rejected in the House of Representatives, which fuelled concerns of a government shutdown.

Despite increasing worries regarding the economy, earnings this week painted a rather better picture, with 14 out of 16 S&P 500 companies beating EPS estimates.

Among the eight consumer companies that reported earnings this week, five of them beat estimates for both revenue and EPS. Underscoring the remarkable growth in the cruise sector after COVID-19 nearly capsized the industry, Carnival Corporation (CCL)’s full year 2024 revenues hit an all-time high and profits swelled by 2500% from a loss of $0.06 in 2023 to a profit of $1.44 in 2024.

Another big name in the sector, Nike (NKE) delivered a better-than-feared FQ2 earnings report, leaving investors to bet on early signs of a turnaround under the company’s new CEO. Despite the beat on estimates, Nike’s revenue decreased 8% year-over-year on a reported basis, with declines seen across the North America, Europe Middle East & Africa, and Greater China regions.

Out of the companies in the Industrial sector, parcel delivery giant FedEx (FDX) shone through, surprising investors with the news of the separation of FedEx Freight, the business that handles large cargo. The separation will create two independent publicly listed companies as part of the company’s streamlining efforts.

On the earnings front, FedEx (FDX) disappointed investors with mixed results and a lowered forecast. Revenue fell about 1% to $22B and missed the consensus estimate of $22.15B. Adjusted EPS is now forecast to be around $19.00 and $20.00 per share, vs. the prior forecast of $20.00 to $21.00 per share.

Among the three tech sector companies that reported earnings this week, Accenture (ACN) and Jabil (JBL) cheered investors, with better than expected results and upbeat forecast.

Accenture (ACN)’s first quarter revenue climbed 9% (in U.S. dollar terms) year-on-year to $17.7B, surpassing analysts' estimates. The company also raised its revenue growth outlook for fiscal 2025.

Micron (MU) on the other hand, despite posting earnings in-line with consensus, disappointed investors as it issued an outlook for the fiscal second-quarter that was well below expectations by a wide margin.

Looking to the next quarter, Micron expects adjusted earnings to be between $1.33 and $1.53 per share, well below the $1.92 per share estimate. Sales are forecast to be between $7.7B and $8.1B, with the midpoint well below the $8.99B estimate.

data.title: Boeing Chief Information Officer Susan Doniz is leaving: report
data.description:
Boeing (NYSE:BA) Chief Information Officer Susan Doniz is leaving the company, Bloomberg reported Saturday, citing a memo to employees by Chief Executive Kelly Ortberg.

Doniz’s departure is effective immediately, the report said.

Ortberg reportedly said in the memo that Dennis Eng will lead the Information Technology & Data Analytics team on an interim basis until a successor for Doniz is named.

Doniz joined Boeing in 2020 from Australian airline Quantas (OTCPK:QABSY)(OTCPK:QUBSF), where she had a similar post.

Her exit follows chief lobbyist Ziad Ojakli’s departure earlier this month.

data.title: Odds now nearly one in five the Fed does nothing next year
data.description:
The Federal Reserve on Wednesday sent out a clear message that it was prepared to slow the pace of interest rate cuts in 2025, roiling U.S. stocks and forcing monetary policy watchers to recalibrate their expectations. Notably, the odds that the central bank will do nothing at all next year have jumped up to nearly 20%.

According to the CME FedWatch tool, the probability of the federal funds rate staying pat at its current level of 4.25%-4.50% at the Dec 10, 2025 monetary policy committee meeting stands at 16.7%. That figure was about 9% a week ago.

Fed chair Jerome Powell on Wednesday cited inflation as one of the primary reasons for the revised rate cut expectations. He observed that the Fed's year-end projection for inflation had "fallen apart" into the end of 2024.

"Ascertaining what level of policy restraint achieves the careful balance of keeping inflation in check and easing stress on interest-rate sensitive sectors will dominate the monetary policy discussion come 2025," Wells Fargo said on Friday.

Powell noted that the quarter-point rate cut delivered on Wednesday was a "closer call" than in November. There was a lone dissent in the decision from Cleveland Fed President Beth Hammack, who voted to keep the key policy rate unchanged.

The Fed had kicked off its policy easing cycle with a supersized half-point rate cut in September. Economic data since then has continued to show an economy that is in robust shape, and progress on inflation that gone sideways - indicators that an easing Fed would not want to see.

"The Fed cut rates today, but won't cut as much as people think tomorrow. Investors should not be surprised. The U.S. economy is in great shape, and the Trump administration is about to introduce a combination of stimulus and supply bottlenecks. Until recently, many economists still expected 3-4 Fed cuts in 2025," Alberto Gallo, chief investment officer at Andromeda Capital Management, said on X (formerly Twitter) on Wednesday.

"The (Fed) made a policy error cutting 50bp (in September), without an emergency and just ahead of a Trump win - as we discussed over the past few months. Today's hawkish cut is an attempt to back track on the market's dovish extrapolation of quarterly cuts despite inflation staying well above target," Gallo added.

For investors looking to track Wall Street's benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500), here are some exchange-traded funds of interest: (SPY), (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), and (SPXU).

  • Wall Street suffers worst week in over a month after Fed delivers a reality check

data.title: Honda, Nissan to considering producing vehicles at each other's factories: report
As quantum computing stocks soar, Wall Street analysts take bullish stance
data.description:
Quantum computing stocks have become the talk of the town after several major updates in the space, including a "mind-boggling" quantum computing chip by Google, which has led to Wall Street analysts also taking note of the companies in the segment.

Shares of quantum computing related stocks have been surging this year. Year-to-date, Quantum Computing (QUBT) has skyrocketed over 1,500%, Rigetti Computing (RGTI) has soared over 650%, and D-Wave Quantum (QBTS) has gained more than 600%. IonQ has added more than 200%, while Arqit Quantum (ARQQ) has jumped over 125% (data as of Dec. 19).

Much has happened in the quantum space. Earlier this month, Alphabet's (GOOG) (GOOGL) unit Google unveiled a quantum computing chip, known as Willow — which is capable of handling complex calculations in a span of five minutes, while the world's most powerful supercomputers would need 10 septillion years.

Last month, Amazon Web Services (AMZN) announcing its Quantum Embark Program also helped the stocks to skyrocket., while earlier this week, Hoboken, N.J.-based Quantum Computing (QUBT) recently saw a contract from the National Aeronautics and Space Administration, or NASA.

Wall Street Sentiment

Wall Street analysts have also jumped into the debate about quantum computing, and have largely started with bullish views with a hint of caution.

D.A. Davidson began coverage of IonQ (IONQ) with a Buy rating and $50 price target. The analysts said they believe IonQ represents a compelling pure-play investment positioned to capitalize on quantum computing's rapid growth, driven by the growing inadequacy of classical computing for solving complex problems.

The firm said that by advancing hardware, networking, and software simultaneously, IonQ is emerging as a full-stack quantum computing leader which is poised to address the next generation of computational workloads.

However, D.A. Davidson said that IonQ's rating initiation was its inaugural coverage under its DaVinci initiative, in which the firm uses an early-stage venture approach focusing on technology and opportunity over financials and valuation metrics, making these recommendations far more speculative than the rest of its coverage.

Analyst David Williams from Benchmark reiterated the firm's Buy rating on IonQ and raised the price target on the shares to $50 from $20, saying that he sees many interesting areas in quantum to participate, but few as attractive as IonQ.

Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating system has a Hold rating on IonQ and so is the Seeking Alpha authors' average rating. Meanwhile, the average Wall Street analysts' rating on the stock is Buy.

Berkeley, Calif.-based Rigetti Computing (RGTI) was initiated at Craig-Hallum with a Buy rating and $12 price target. Analysts led by Richard Shannon said Rigetti and other quantum computing names, in the recent weeks, have been caught up in a retail-driven fervor. The analysts noted that this environment creates risks for investors in the near-term, which they do not downplay, but provided other perspectives too.

The analysts think there is scarcity value in the public markets for quantum computing plays. As such, they think, Rigetti and quantum computing deserve a place in investors' portfolios today.

Quant Rating gives Rigetti a Strong Buy and so does the average Wall Street analysts' rating. The Seeking Alpha authors' average rating is Hold.

Benchmark's Analyst David Williams increased D-Wave Quantum's (QBTS) price target to $8 from $3. The analyst, among other things, said that D-Wave is positioned to benefit from the National Quantum Initiative Reauthorization Act over the next five years and sees room for upside if the company gains traction in hardware sales.

The SA Quant rating on D-Wave Quantum (QBTS) is Hold, while the SA authors' average rating is Buy. The average Wall Street analysts' rating is Strong Buy.

The Quantum Computing's (QUBT) average Wall Street analysts' rating and SA Quant rating, both, is Strong Buy. The SA authors' average rating is Buy.

The SA Quant rating and the SA authors' average rating, both, on Arqit Quantum (ARQQ) is Hold, while the average Wall Street analysts' (total 1 analyst in this case) rating is Buy.
